title: Minimum Wage Schedule 2024: Rajasthan
content: Under the Minimum Wages Act 1948, the state government of Rajasthan sets the statutory minimum wage for scheduled employments. As of the 2024 notification, an unskilled worker must receive at least ₹8500 per month. A skilled or highly skilled worker must receive at least ₹11000 per month, excluding discretionary allowances. Paying below these rates violates Section 22 of the Minimum Wages Act and is a criminal offence.

title: Professional Tax: Rajasthan (No PT Levied)
content: Rajasthan has not enacted a Professional Tax law. Employers operating in Rajasthan must not deduct any Professional Tax from employees' salaries. Any such deduction would be unlawful.

title: Minimum Wage Schedule 2024: Bihar
content: Under the Minimum Wages Act 1948, the state government of Bihar sets the statutory minimum wage for scheduled employments. As of the 2024 notification, an unskilled worker must receive at least ₹9000 per month. A skilled or highly skilled worker must receive at least ₹11500 per month, excluding discretionary allowances. Paying below these rates violates Section 22 of the Minimum Wages Act and is a criminal offence.

title: Professional Tax Schedule: Bihar
content: Bihar levies Professional Tax under Article 276(2) of the Constitution. The maximum annual ceiling is ₹2,500. Monthly deduction slabs are: Salary ≤25,000: ₹0/month. Salary >25,000: ₹208/month. Employers must deduct PT from each employee's salary and remit to the state government. Failure to deduct or remit PT is an offence under the state PT Act.

title: Minimum Wage Schedule 2024: Madhya Pradesh
content: Under the Minimum Wages Act 1948, the state government of Madhya Pradesh sets the statutory minimum wage for scheduled employments. As of the 2024 notification, an unskilled worker must receive at least ₹9500 per month. A skilled or highly skilled worker must receive at least ₹12500 per month, excluding discretionary allowances. Paying below these rates violates Section 22 of the Minimum Wages Act and is a criminal offence.

title: Professional Tax Schedule: Madhya Pradesh
content: Madhya Pradesh levies Professional Tax under Article 276(2) of the Constitution. The maximum annual ceiling is ₹2,500. Monthly deduction slabs are: Salary ≤18,750: ₹0/month. Salary >18,750: ₹208/month. Employers must deduct PT from each employee's salary and remit to the state government. Failure to deduct or remit PT is an offence under the state PT Act.

title: Minimum Wage Schedule 2024: Odisha
content: Under the Minimum Wages Act 1948, the state government of Odisha sets the statutory minimum wage for scheduled employments. As of the 2024 notification, an unskilled worker must receive at least ₹9800 per month. A skilled or highly skilled worker must receive at least ₹13000 per month, excluding discretionary allowances. Paying below these rates violates Section 22 of the Minimum Wages Act and is a criminal offence.

title: Professional Tax Schedule: Odisha
content: Odisha levies Professional Tax under Article 276(2) of the Constitution. The maximum annual ceiling is ₹2,500. Monthly deduction slabs are: Salary ≤13,304: ₹0/month. Salary 13,305–25,000: ₹125/month. Salary >25,000: ₹200/month. Employers must deduct PT from each employee's salary and remit to the state government. Failure to deduct or remit PT is an offence under the state PT Act.

title: EPF Contribution Rate — Section 6
content: Under Section 6 of the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 1952, both the employee and employer must each contribute 12% of the employee's basic wages. Of the employer's 12%, 8.33% is diverted to the Employees' Pension Scheme (EPS) and 3.67% goes to the EPF account. The wage ceiling for mandatory EPF/EPS contributions is ₹15,000 per month.

title: EPF Wage Ceiling — Para 26(6)
content: The statutory wage ceiling for EPF/EPS contributions is ₹15,000 per month. Employers are not required to contribute on wages above ₹15,000 unless a joint option under Para 26(6) is exercised. Employees earning above ₹15,000 may voluntarily opt to contribute on their full wages by submitting a joint request with their employer within 6 months of joining.

title: ESI Wage Ceiling and Contribution Rates
content: The Employees' State Insurance (ESI) scheme applies to employees whose gross salary is ₹21,000 per month or less (₹25,000 for employees with disabilities). The ESI contribution rates are: employee 0.75% of gross wages, employer 3.25% of gross wages. ESI applies to establishments with 10 or more employees (20 in some states). Employees earning above ₹21,000 gross are not covered by ESI.

title: Code on Wages 2019 — Wage Definition and 50% Basic Rule
content: Under Section 2(y) of the Code on Wages 2019, 'wages' includes basic pay, dearness allowance, and retaining allowance. The Code requires that the Basic Pay component must constitute at least 50% of the total gross salary. If an employer structures the salary such that Basic Pay falls below 50% of gross, the excess allowances are treated as wages for EPF/ESI computation purposes. This prevents employers from artificially suppressing PF contributions.

title: Payment of Bonus Act 1965 — Eligibility and Calculation
content: Under the Payment of Bonus Act 1965, every employee earning up to ₹21,000 per month is eligible for a statutory bonus. The minimum bonus is 8.33% of annual basic salary (or ₹100, whichever is higher). The maximum bonus payable is 20% of annual basic salary. Employees whose basic salary exceeds ₹7,000 per month or the minimum wage (whichever is higher) have the bonus calculated on ₹7,000 for the purposes of the Act. The employer must pay bonus within 8 months of the close of the accounting year.

title: Payment of Gratuity Act 1972 — Eligibility and Calculation
content: Under Section 4 of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity is payable to an employee on termination of service after completing a minimum of 5 years of continuous service. The 5-year rule is waived in cases of death or disability. Calculation formula: (Last Drawn Monthly Salary × 15 × Years of Service) ÷ 26. 'Last drawn salary' means basic wages plus dearness allowance. The maximum gratuity payable is ₹20 Lakhs. Gratuity is payable within 30 days of becoming due; the employer must pay simple interest at 10% per annum for any delay.

title: Gratuity Eligibility — 4 Years 8 Months Service (Madras HC)
content: The Madras High Court has held that an employee who completes 4 years and 240 days (approximately 4 years and 8 months) of service qualifies for gratuity, as 240 days constitutes a full year of service for establishments operating 6 days per week. For 5-day-week establishments, the threshold is 190 days in the fifth year. This judicial interpretation is widely followed and effectively reduces the practical threshold from 5 full calendar years.

title: Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act 2017 — Leave Entitlement
content: Under the Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act 2017, a female employee is entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave for the first two live births, and 12 weeks for subsequent births. Eligibility requires 80 days of actual work in the 12 months preceding the expected delivery. The benefit applies to all establishments with 10 or more employees. Paid maternity leave also applies in cases of miscarriage (6 weeks), medical termination of pregnancy (6 weeks), and tubectomy (2 weeks). Employers with 50 or more employees must provide creche facilities.

title: Industrial Disputes Act 1947 — Section 25F Retrenchment Compensation
content: Under Section 25F of the Industrial Disputes Act 1947, an employer cannot retrench a workman who has completed one year of continuous service without: (a) giving one month's written notice or pay in lieu; (b) paying retrenchment compensation at 15 days' average pay for every completed year of service; and (c) giving notice to the appropriate government authority. Retrenchment without these steps is unlawful. An aggrieved employee can file a complaint before the Labour Court within 3 years of the retrenchment.

title: Industrial Disputes Act — Section 25G (Last In First Out Rule)
content: Section 25G of the Industrial Disputes Act 1947 requires that an employer retrenching workmen in any category must retrench the person most recently employed in that category first (last-in-first-out principle), unless there are valid reasons for departing from this order. Deviation from this rule without reasons constitutes unlawful discrimination in retrenchment.

title: Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946 — Notice Period
content: Under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act 1946, certified standing orders for a factory or establishment must specify the notice period required for termination. For permanent workmen, the typical minimum notice period is 30 days. If the offer letter or contract specifies a longer notice period (e.g., 60 or 90 days), that contractual period applies. An employer who terminates without giving the agreed notice must pay wages in lieu of notice. An employee who resigns without serving notice may have pay deducted for the shortfall.

title: Factories Act 1948 — Section 51/59 Overtime Pay
content: Under the Factories Act 1948, no adult worker shall be required or permitted to work for more than 9 hours on any day or 48 hours in any week (Section 51). Under Section 59, if a worker works more than 9 hours a day or 48 hours a week, they must be paid overtime at twice the ordinary rate of wages. The overtime limit is 60 hours per week and 50 hours in any quarter. Violations are punishable under Section 92 of the Act.

title: Shops and Establishments Acts — Overtime for Non-Factory Workers
content: Workers in shops, commercial establishments, and offices are governed by the respective state Shops and Establishments Act. Most state acts cap daily working hours at 9 hours and weekly hours at 48 hours. Overtime beyond these limits must be compensated at 1.5x to 2x the ordinary rate, depending on the state act. Employers cannot compel workers to work overtime without their consent in most states.

title: POSH Act 2013 — Internal Complaints Committee Mandate
content: Under the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act 2013, every employer with 10 or more employees is required to constitute an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC). The ICC must have a presiding officer who is a senior female employee, at least two other employees, and one member from an NGO or women's association. The ICC must complete enquiry within 60 days of receiving a complaint.

title: POSH Act 2013 — Local Complaints Committee for Small Establishments
content: For workplaces with fewer than 10 employees or where a complaint is against the employer directly, complaints are filed with the Local Complaints Committee (LCC) constituted by the District Officer. The LCC has the same powers as an ICC. Employees of unorganised sector workplaces and domestic workers can also file complaints with the LCC.

title: Code on Social Security 2020 — Gig and Platform Worker Coverage
content: The Code on Social Security 2020 consolidates 9 central labour laws including the EPF Act, ESI Act, Maternity Benefit Act, and Gratuity Act. Chapter IX of the Code specifically recognises gig workers (those engaged in task-based work outside the traditional employer-employee relationship) and platform workers (those working through digital platforms). The Code requires state governments to set up Social Security Funds for gig and platform workers, funded by contributions from aggregator platforms. Benefits include life and disability cover, accident insurance, and health and maternity benefits.

title: Industrial Relations Code 2020 — Retrenchment Threshold
content: The Industrial Relations Code 2020 consolidates the Industrial Disputes Act, the Trade Unions Act, and the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act. Under the Code, establishments with 300 or more workers require prior government permission before retrenchment or closure. Establishments with fewer than 300 workers can retrench workers by following the notice and compensation provisions without seeking prior permission. This threshold was raised from 100 workers under the Industrial Disputes Act.

title: Relieving Letter — Employer's Obligation
content: There is no specific central statute mandating that an employer must issue a relieving letter. However, withholding a relieving letter as coercion or to prevent future employment may be challenged as restraint of trade under Section 27 of the Indian Contract Act 1872. An employee can approach the Labour Commissioner or file a civil suit for recovery of the relieving letter. Several High Courts have directed employers to issue relieving letters when withheld without valid cause.

title: Notice Period Buy-Out — Employee Resignation Before Notice Expires
content: When an employee resigns and is unable or unwilling to serve the full notice period specified in the employment contract or standing orders, the employer may allow a 'notice buy-out' — the employee pays the employer wages equivalent to the shortfall in notice days. The amount is typically calculated as (Basic + DA) ÷ 30 × remaining notice days. The employer cannot withhold the full and final settlement solely because of a notice period dispute; any recovery must be through civil proceedings or agreed deduction from F&F. An employee cannot be forced to serve the notice period against their will — specific performance of a service contract is barred under Section 14 of the Specific Relief Act 1963.

title: Notice Period Waiver — Employer's Right to Relieve Early
content: An employer may, at its discretion, waive the notice period and relieve the employee immediately or earlier than the contractual notice end date. In this case, the employer must pay the employee salary for the full notice period (pay in lieu of notice) unless the employee voluntarily agrees to forgo it. If the employer relieves the employee early without notice pay, the employee can recover the shortfall as unpaid wages under the Payment of Wages Act 1936 or through a civil suit. Early relieving by mutual consent is the cleanest route and should be documented in writing.

title: Wrongful Termination — Employee Remedies and Forum Selection
content: An employee who has been wrongfully terminated has several remedies depending on their category: (1) Workmen (as defined under the Industrial Disputes Act) can raise an industrial dispute before the Labour Commissioner, and if unresolved, before the Labour Court, which can award reinstatement with back wages. (2) Managerial or supervisory employees (not 'workmen') must approach civil courts or the High Court; they cannot use Labour Courts. (3) Employees in government or public sector establishments may have access to service tribunals or departmental appeals. Filing a complaint under the ID Act must be done within 3 years of the wrongful termination. If the employer is in an 'essential service', different notice requirements apply.

title: Termination for Misconduct — Effect on Gratuity
content: Under Section 4(6) of the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, gratuity can be forfeited wholly or partially if an employee's services are terminated due to: (a) willful omission or negligence causing damage or loss to the employer's property; or (b) riotous or disorderly conduct or any other act of violence. Gratuity can also be wholly forfeited if the termination is due to an offence involving moral turpitude. Termination for ordinary misconduct (e.g., poor performance, insubordination without violence) does NOT forfeit gratuity — the employee remains entitled to gratuity if they have completed 5 years of service. The employer must follow a disciplinary inquiry and issue a charge sheet before forfeiting gratuity.

title: Garden Leave During Notice Period
content: Garden leave (also called 'gardening leave') is a practice where an employer asks an employee to stay away from the workplace during the notice period while continuing to pay their full salary. This is legally permissible if the employment contract or standing orders allow it. During garden leave, the employee's employment continues — they are still entitled to salary, benefits, and statutory entitlements such as EPF contributions. Garden leave clauses are common in senior roles and IT/financial sector contracts where the employer wants to prevent the employee from immediately joining a competitor. The employee cannot be compelled to work if placed on garden leave.

title: New Tax Regime — Default Tax Slabs (FY 2024-25)
content: Under Section 115BAC of the Income Tax Act, as amended by the Finance Act 2023, the new tax regime is the default for individuals from FY 2023-24 onward. The tax slabs under the new regime are: Income up to ₹3 lakh — Nil; ₹3–6 lakh — 5%; ₹6–9 lakh — 10%; ₹9–12 lakh — 15%; ₹12–15 lakh — 20%; Above ₹15 lakh — 30%. A standard deduction of ₹50,000 is available under the new regime. Under Section 87A, a tax rebate of up to ₹25,000 is available if total income does not exceed ₹7 lakh, making effective tax Nil up to ₹7 lakh.

title: Old Tax Regime — Tax Slabs (FY 2024-25)
content: Under the old tax regime (opt-in), the tax slabs for individuals below 60 years are: Income up to ₹2.5 lakh — Nil; ₹2.5–5 lakh — 5%; ₹5–10 lakh — 20%; Above ₹10 lakh — 30%. Standard deduction of ₹50,000 is available. Under Section 87A, a rebate of up to ₹12,500 applies if total income does not exceed ₹5 lakh. The old regime allows deductions under Sections 80C (₹1.5 lakh), 80D, HRA exemption, LTA exemption, and other Chapter VI-A deductions, which can significantly reduce taxable income.

title: House Rent Allowance — Section 10(13A) Exemption
content: Under Section 10(13A) of the Income Tax Act, the HRA exemption is the minimum of three amounts: (1) Actual HRA received from employer; (2) Rent paid minus 10% of basic salary; (3) 50% of basic salary for metro cities (Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai) or 40% for non-metro cities. This exemption is available only under the old tax regime. Employees under the new tax regime cannot claim HRA exemption. If rent paid exceeds ₹1 lakh per annum (₹8,333 per month), the landlord's PAN must be provided to the employer.

title: Section 80C — Deductions up to ₹1.5 Lakh (Old Regime Only)
content: Under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, a deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakh per annum is available from taxable income under the old tax regime. Eligible investments include: Employee's own EPF contribution, PPF, ELSS (Equity Linked Savings Scheme), NSC (National Savings Certificate), life insurance premiums, home loan principal repayment, tuition fees for two children, Sukanya Samriddhi, 5-year bank fixed deposits, and NPS Tier-1 contributions. This deduction is not available under the new tax regime.

title: Section 80D — Medical Insurance Premium Deduction
content: Under Section 80D of the Income Tax Act (old regime only), deductions are available for health insurance premiums paid. For self, spouse, and dependent children: deduction up to ₹25,000 per year (₹50,000 if the insured is a senior citizen). For parents: additional deduction up to ₹25,000 per year (₹50,000 if parents are senior citizens). Maximum total deduction possible: ₹1 lakh (if self is senior citizen and parents are senior citizens). Preventive health check-up expenses up to ₹5,000 are included within these limits.

title: Section 80E — Education Loan Interest Deduction
content: Under Section 80E of the Income Tax Act (old regime only), interest paid on an education loan taken for higher education is fully deductible for up to 8 years (starting from the year repayment begins), or until the interest is fully repaid, whichever is earlier. There is no upper limit on the deduction amount. The loan must be taken from a financial institution or approved charitable institution for full-time courses after Class 12. The deduction applies to the borrower (student or parent).

title: Section 80G — Deduction for Charitable Donations
content: Under Section 80G of the Income Tax Act (available under old regime; not under new regime), donations to approved funds and institutions are deductible. Donations to certain funds (PM National Relief Fund, Chief Minister's Relief Fund) qualify for 100% deduction without any limit. Donations to other approved institutions qualify for 50% deduction, subject to a limit of 10% of adjusted gross total income. Cash donations above ₹2,000 are not eligible.

title: Section 192A — TDS on EPF Withdrawal
content: Under Section 192A of the Income Tax Act, TDS is deducted on EPF withdrawal when both conditions are met: (1) the employee's total service is less than 5 years, and (2) the withdrawal amount exceeds ₹50,000. TDS rates: 10% if valid PAN is provided; 20% if PAN is not provided. TDS does not apply if: service is 5 or more years, the amount is ₹50,000 or less, the claim is a transfer (not withdrawal), the claim is a partial advance (Form 31), or the employee submits Form 15G/15H (if eligible). Combined service across employers counts if PF was transferred.

title: Form 16 — TDS Certificate for Salary Income
content: Form 16 is a TDS certificate issued by the employer to the employee by 15 June of the assessment year. Part A contains details of TDS deposited with the government (quarterly). Part B contains the salary breakup, allowances, perquisites, and deductions claimed. Form 16 is the primary document needed to file Income Tax Return (ITR-1 or ITR-2) for salaried employees. If Form 16 is not issued by the employer, the employee can still file ITR using Form 26AS (tax credit statement), pay slips, and bank statements. An employer who fails to issue Form 16 can be penalised under Section 272A.

title: Section 44ADA — Presumptive Tax Scheme for Professionals and Freelancers
content: Under Section 44ADA of the Income Tax Act, professionals (doctors, lawyers, engineers, accountants, architects, consultants, etc.) and freelancers with gross receipts up to ₹75 lakh (₹75 lakh from FY 2023-24) can opt for the presumptive taxation scheme. Under this scheme, 50% of gross receipts is deemed to be the taxable profit — no books of accounts or expense proof is required. The remaining 50% is treated as expenses. This option is available to resident individuals and partnership firms. Tax is calculated on the presumptive income at the applicable slab rates.

title: Section 194J — TDS on Professional and Technical Fees
content: Under Section 194J of the Income Tax Act, any person paying fees for professional services, technical services, royalties, or non-compete fees to a resident must deduct TDS at 10% on amounts exceeding ₹30,000 per annum. For purely technical services (not professional), the rate is 2% from FY 2020-21. Freelancers and consultants whose clients deduct TDS under Section 194J will receive Form 16A as a TDS certificate and can claim this TDS credit in their ITR.

title: Section 115BBH — Tax on Virtual Digital Assets (Crypto)
content: Under Section 115BBH of the Income Tax Act (introduced from FY 2022-23), income from transfer of any Virtual Digital Asset (VDA), including cryptocurrency and NFTs, is taxed at a flat rate of 30% (plus 4% cess = 31.2%), regardless of the holding period or the investor's income slab. No deductions except the cost of acquisition are allowed. Losses from VDAs cannot be set off against other income. Additionally, a 1% TDS is deducted by the exchange under Section 194S on VDA transactions above ₹10,000 per year.

title: Capital Gains Tax — STCG and LTCG on Equity
content: For equity shares and equity mutual funds listed on a recognised stock exchange: Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG, held ≤ 1 year) are taxed at 20% under Section 111A (revised from 15% by Finance Act 2024, effective 23 July 2024). Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG, held > 1 year) exceeding ₹1.25 lakh per year are taxed at 12.5% under Section 112A (revised from 10%, ₹1 lakh exemption). For other assets (property, gold, debt funds): STCG is taxed as per slab rates; LTCG (held > 2 years for property, > 3 years for debt funds) is taxed at 20% with indexation benefit.

title: Income Tax Refund — Status Check and Common Issues
content: Income tax refunds are issued after ITR processing by the Centralised Processing Centre (CPC) in Bengaluru. To check refund status: visit incometax.gov.in > Login > e-File > Income Tax Returns > View Filed Returns, or use the NSDL portal at tin.tin.nsdl.com. Common reasons for refund failure: bank account not validated/pre-validated on the IT portal, bank account IFSC changed or closed, PAN-bank account mismatch. To resolve: pre-validate the bank account on the IT portal under 'My Profile > Bank Account'. Refunds are typically credited within 20–45 days of ITR processing.

title: Old Tax Regime — Detailed Slab Table by Age Category (FY 2024-25)
content: Under the old tax regime (opt-in for FY 2024-25), income tax slabs differ by taxpayer category. For individuals below 60 years: up to ₹2.5 lakh — Nil; ₹2,50,001–₹5 lakh — 5%; ₹5,00,001–₹10 lakh — 20%; above ₹10 lakh — 30%. For senior citizens (60–79 years): up to ₹3 lakh — Nil; ₹3–5 lakh — 5%; ₹5–10 lakh — 20%; above ₹10 lakh — 30%. For super senior citizens (80+ years): up to ₹5 lakh — Nil; ₹5–10 lakh — 20%; above ₹10 lakh — 30%. Health and Education Cess of 4% is levied on total tax in all categories. Surcharge applies at 10% for income 50 lakh–1 crore; 15% for 1–2 crore; 25% for 2–5 crore; 37% above 5 crore (marginal relief available). The 87A rebate (up to ₹12,500) applies if total income does not exceed ₹5 lakh.

title: Income Tax Refund Delay — Escalation and Grievance Process
content: If an income tax refund is not credited within 45 days of ITR processing, the taxpayer can escalate through these steps: (1) Check AIS/26AS to confirm TDS credit is correctly reflected. (2) Raise a refund re-issue request on the e-filing portal under 'Services > Refund Reissue' if the bank account was not pre-validated or account details were wrong. (3) File a grievance on the e-filing portal under 'e-Nivaran / Grievance' with the processing reference number. (4) For ITRs processed by CPC Bengaluru, use the CPC helpline at 1800-103-0025. Refund interest under Section 244A accrues at 0.5% per month from April 1 of the assessment year (or from the date of filing if filed after April 1) until the date the refund is granted.

title: Income Tax Refund Adjusted Against Outstanding Demand
content: Under Section 245 of the Income Tax Act, the Income Tax Department can adjust an income tax refund against any outstanding tax demand from prior assessment years. Before making such an adjustment, the department must serve a notice to the taxpayer. If the taxpayer disputes the outstanding demand, they must respond to the notice within 30 days. Steps to resolve: (1) Log in to the e-filing portal > Pending Actions > Response to Outstanding Demand — submit a response agreeing or disagreeing with the demand. (2) If the demand is incorrect, upload documentary evidence. (3) If the demand arises from a previous ITR, verify via 26AS whether TDS was correctly credited for that year. Failure to respond to the 245 notice results in the refund being automatically adjusted.

title: New Tax Regime — Slab Table with Surcharge and Cess (FY 2024-25)
content: Under the new tax regime (default from FY 2023-24), the complete tax computation for individuals is: Income up to ₹3 lakh — Nil; ₹3–7 lakh — 5% (rebate under 87A makes this Nil for total income ≤ ₹7 lakh); ₹7–10 lakh — 10%; ₹10–12 lakh — 15%; ₹12–15 lakh — 20%; above ₹15 lakh — 30%. Standard deduction of ₹75,000 is available from FY 2024-25 (revised from ₹50,000 by Finance Act 2024). Surcharge: 10% for income 50 lakh–1 crore; 15% for 1–2 crore; 25% for 2–5 crore; 25% above 5 crore (capped at 25% — reduced from 37% under new regime, effective FY 2023-24). Cess: 4% on tax plus surcharge. The effective tax on ₹7 lakh income under new regime is Nil after 87A rebate of ₹25,000.
